org_constraint: The email must be written in the first person perspective.
new_constraint: Write the email in the second person perspective.

Please note that grammatically, a single narrative voice cannot be exclusively in both the first person ("I") and the second person ("You") simultaneously, as these refer to the writer and the reader respectively. However, in professional correspondence, it is standard to write from the **first-person perspective** (as the sender) while addressing the recipient using the **second-person perspective**.

Below is a draft that successfully integrates both perspectives naturally to meet your requirements.

***

**Subject:** Introduction and Research Interests – [Your Full Name]

Dear Professor [Professor’s Last Name],

I hope this email finds you well. **I am writing** to formally introduce myself as a prospective undergraduate/graduate student at [University Name] who is deeply interested in your work.

**My name is** [Your Full Name], and I am currently pursuing [Degree] in [Major/Field]. Throughout my academic career, **I have focused** on [Specific Topic/Goal], and **I believe** this aligns closely with the research you are leading at [Department/Lab Name].

**You have published** several seminal papers regarding [Specific Paper/Topic], which particularly inspired **my interest**. **I am eager to learn more** about how **your team is approaching** [Current Project/Problem]. **My ultimate goal is** to conduct independent research in this field and eventually contribute to similar breakthroughs.

If **your schedule allows**, **I would appreciate** the opportunity to speak with **you** briefly or review your feedback on my attached resume. **Thank you for your time**, and **I look forward to hearing** from **you**.

Sincerely,

[Your Full Name]
[Your Contact Information]
[Link to LinkedIn/Portfolio, optional]<|im_end|>

final_answer:
Please note that grammatically, a single narrative voice cannot be exclusively in both the first person ("I") and the second person ("You") simultaneously, as these refer to the writer and the reader respectively. However, in professional correspondence, it is standard to write from the **first-person perspective** (as the sender) while addressing the recipient using the **second-person perspective**.

Below is a draft that successfully integrates both perspectives naturally to meet your requirements.

***

**Subject:** Introduction and Research Interests – [Your Full Name]

Dear Professor [Professor’s Last Name],

I hope this email finds you well. **I am writing** to formally introduce myself as a prospective undergraduate/graduate student at [University Name] who is deeply interested in your work.

**My name is** [Your Full Name], and I am currently pursuing [Degree] in [Major/Field]. Throughout my academic career, **I have focused** on [Specific Topic/Goal], and **I believe** this aligns closely with the research you are leading at [Department/Lab Name].

**You have published** several seminal papers regarding [Specific Paper/Topic], which particularly inspired **my interest**. **I am eager to learn more** about how **your team is approaching** [Current Project/Problem]. **My ultimate goal is** to conduct independent research in this field and eventually contribute to similar breakthroughs.

If **your schedule allows**, **I would appreciate** the opportunity to speak with **you** briefly or review your feedback on my attached resume. **Thank you for your time**, and **I look forward to hearing** from **you**.

Sincerely,

[Your Full Name]
[Your Contact Information]
[Link to LinkedIn/Portfolio, optional]<|im_end|>
